paper_title: Agent Explorative Policy Optimization for Multimodal Agentic Reasoning

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Vision-language models with extended reasoning succeed on complex problems, but many real-world problems require external tools that internal reasoning alone often cannot resolve. Agentic reasoning therefore interleaves two behaviors with a structural asymmetry: thinking (the self-contained default) and tool use (a high-variance auxiliary acting). We refer to this asymmetry as the Thinking-Acting Gap. Under standard RL recipes like GRPO, the gap manifests as two diagnostic symptoms during training: tool use is attempted on only ~30% of rollouts, and when attempted, the tool-using rollouts within a group are all-wrong on ~40% of questions, suppressing the learning signal at the tool calls that needed it. We propose AXPO (Agent eXplorative Policy Optimization): for each all-wrong tool-using subgroup, AXPO fixes the thinking prefix and resamples the tool call and its continuation, paired with uncertainty-based prefix selection. Across nine multimodal benchmarks and three scales of Qwen3-VL-Thinking, SFT+AXPO outperforms SFT+GRPO at average (+1.8pp Pass@1 and +1.8pp Pass@4 at 8B on average) and 8B with SFT+AXPO surpasses the 32B Base on Pass@4 with 4 times fewer parameters.

Agent Explorative Policy Optimization for
Multimodal Agentic Reasoning
sections.content: Minki Kang1,2*, Shizhe Diao1, Ryo Hachiuma1, Sung Ju Hwang2, Pavlo Molchanov1, Yu-Chiang Frank Wang1, Byung-Kwan Lee1 1NVIDIA 2KAIST Abstract Vision-language models with extended reasoning succeed on complex problems, but many real-world problems require external tools that internal reasoning alone often cannot resolve. Agentic reasoning therefore interleaves two behaviors with structural asymmetry: thinking (the self-contained default) and tool use (a high-variance auxiliary acting). We refer to this asymmetry as the Thinking-Acting Gap. Under standard RL recipes like GRPO, the gap manifests as two diagnostic symptoms during training: tool use is attempted on only 30% of rollouts, and when attempted, the tool-using rollouts within group are all-wrong on 40% of questions, suppressing the learning signal at the tool calls that needed it. We propose AXPO (Agent eXplorative Policy Optimization): for each all-wrong tool-using subgroup, AXPO fixes the thinking prefix and resamples the tool call and its continuation, paired with uncertainty-based prefix selection. Across nine multimodal benchmarks and three scales of Qwen3-VL-Thinking, SFT + AXPO outperforms SFT + GRPO at average (+1.8 pp Pass@1 and +1.8 pp Pass@4 at 8B on average) and 8B with SFT + AXPO surpasses the 32B Base on Pass@4 with 4 fewer parameters. The project page is available at link. Figure 1 Main result: AXPO narrows the agentic gap to 4 larger baseline. Average Pass@1 (left) and Pass@4 (right) over nine multimodal benchmarks across Qwen3-VL-Thinking sizes. At 8B, AXPO surpasses the 32B Base baseline on Pass@4 while outperforming GRPO at every scale. 1. Introduction Large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) with extended reasoning capabilities have made remarkable progress, producing chains of thought that rival much larger models on mathematical and scientific reasoning benchmarks [13]. These reasoning models invest test-time compute into internal search and reflection to arrive at accurate answers. However, many real-world problems lie beyond what internal reasoning alone can resolve: retrieving up-to-date knowledge, performing complex numerical computation, and understanding fine-grained visual details all require access to external resources [48]. Tools such as web search, code interpreters, and image manipulation utilities can fill this gap, enabling what we call Multimodal Agentic Reasoning: the model autonomously decides when to think further and when to act through tools, much as it *Work done during internship. Project Lead. Correspondence to <minkikang@kaist.ac.kr, byungkwanl@nvidia.com> 2026 NVIDIA. All rights reserved. Agent Explorative Policy Optimization Figure 2 Concept. AXPO concentrates exploration on the tool call for agentic reasoning. Left: under GRPO, tool-using rollouts are scarce and often fail, leaving no positive learning signal at the tool call where it is most needed. Right: AXPO fixes the thinking prefix from the failed rollout and resamples the tool call and its continuation, recovering correct rollout and producing positive learning signal on the tool call. learns to allocate thinking budget, but now extended to interaction with environments [914]. Agentic reasoning carries deeper asymmetry between its two constituent behaviors: Thinking and Tool use (Acting). Thinking is the default mode, self-contained within the models token space: every token is under its control and errors can be corrected by exploring different chain of thought [1517]. Tool use, by contrast, is an auxiliary behavior that is simultaneously powerful but fragile: short token sequence triggers an external response the model cannot predict or control, and small changes in the call can produce drastically different outcomes (e.g., invalid python code with wrong indent) [18]. Reasoning models are heavily post-trained for thinking, but far less for acting. We refer to this asymmetry between safe default and high-variance auxiliary as the Thinking-Acting Gap. Due to this gap, eliciting agentic reasoning remains challenge despite the capabilities of recent reasoning VLMs [19]. Relying solely on prompting is often insufficient to ensure an appropriate tool call when needed. This necessitates post-training pipelines, such as Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL), as evidenced in Figure 1. SFT reliably teaches the mechanics of tool use but struggles to teach the model to utilize tools better for situations the teachers demonstrations did not cover. The resulting agent inherits the teachers failure modes and often plateaus near its performance [20, 21]. Therefore, the model requires training signal that goes beyond imitation, which motivates utilizing RL with verifiable outcome rewards after SFT [18, 22, 23]. However, RL methods such as GRPO [2426] are suboptimal to make models proficient at acting, because two patterns suppress the tool-call learning signal. First, tool use remains minority of rollouts compared with thinking-only (Figure 3a). Second, when tool use is attempted, the entire group of rollouts is far more likely to fail together (Figure 3b). Under GRPO, this asymmetry weakens the tool-call learning signal in both regimes: tool-using rollouts trail thinking-only successes in mixed groups (negative advantage) and zero out in all-wrong groups [27, 28]. These observations motivate targeted exploration for tool use during RL. This raises critical question: how can we effectively induce such exploration? The Thinking-Acting Gap suggests the answer. Thinking is the native mode of the model and the tool call is the separate mode that turns an intent into concrete action where the same intent admits diverse implementations (Figure 3c). Building on this observation, our key insight is that resampling at the tool call lets the model explore diverse agentic reasoning: we anchor the thinking prefix and resample the tool call and its continuation (Figure 2). We call this tool-call resampling. The operation has no analog in pure reasoning, where there is no comparable boundary between committed intent and high-variance 2 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization action. When tool-using trajectory fails, the thinking prefix may be entirely sound. Re-deriving the full trajectory from scratch wastes budget on prefixes that were not the bottleneck, whereas tool-call resampling concentrates exploration precisely where the Thinking-Acting Gap manifests. We propose AXPO (Agent eXplorative Policy Optimization) based on this principle to narrow the gap during RL. Concretely, AXPO instantiates three design choices on top of small extra rollout budget: (1) where to resample, only on all-wrong tool-using subgroups where single recovery flips the GRPO advantage; (2) which prefix, the highest-uncertainty ones; and (3) how resamples enter the gradient, per-prefix GRPO advantage on continuations and binary recovery indicator on the source prefix. Unlike prior reward-shaping methods [18, 23, 29] or rollout scaling methods [30, 31], AXPO restructures the rollout distribution itself at the locus of the Thinking-Acting Gap. Across nine multimodal benchmarks at Qwen3-VL-Thinking 2B/4B/8B [19], SFT + AXPO outperforms SFT + GRPO at average with Pass@4 gains of +2.8/+2.3/+1.8 pp. At 8B, SFT + AXPO surpasses the 32B Base on Pass@4 and reaches 99% of its Pass@1, despite using 4 fewer parameters (Figure 1). The improvement concentrates on tool-intensive benchmarks, while preserving the performance improvement in reasoning benchmarks compared to SFT + GRPO baseline. Notably, +25% extra resampling budget with AXPO delivers +1.1 pp over +100% extra rollout budget with GRPO, confirming that the gain comes from where compute is spent. Our contributions are as follows: We identify the Thinking-Acting Gap, an asymmetry between thinking and tool use that manifests in group-based agentic RL as two symptoms: tool use is attempted on 30% of rollouts and tool-using subgroups (the tool-using rollouts within group) are all-wrong on 40% of questions, yielding non-positive advantage on tool-call tokens. We propose AXPO, an RL algorithm that closes the gap via tool-call resampling: for all-wrong tool-using subgroups, AXPO fixes the thinking prefix at uncertain tool calls and resamples from them, provably dominating from-scratch sampling on tool-use coverage at fixed budget. We validate AXPO on nine multimodal benchmarks across three scales of Qwen3-VL-Thinking, with gains for all sizes compared to GRPO baseline, and the resulting 2B model matches the 8B Base and the 8B model surpasses the 32B Base on Pass@4. 2. Analysis of RL in Agentic Reasoning 2.1. Preliminaries Agentic Reasoning. Diagnosing the Thinking-Acting Gap We now ground the Thinking-Acting Gap in three diagnostic measurements collected during RL training (Figure 3). Our starting point is the SFT checkpoint, trained with GRPO [24] on outcome rewards. Tool use is under-attempted. defining property of agentic reasoning, unlike conventional agentic RL [31, 33], is that tool use is optional: the model can either produce <tool_call> tag or skip straight to <answer>. This optionality makes action fragile under RL. We track the fraction of rollouts that contain at least one tool call over the course of RL. As shown in Figure 3a, which bins each question by its tool-using rollout count among 𝑁 = 8, the corresponding rollout-level tool-use rate is roughly 27% at step 1 and stays in the 2035% range over RL training, well below the thinking-only majority. GRPO exerts no pressure to grow the tool-using minority, and the behavior that distinguishes agentic from non-agentic reasoning remains under-represented in each group. When attempted, the tool-using subgroup is prone to being all-wrong. Within GRPO group of 𝑁 rollouts for one question, we call the rollouts that emit tool call the tool-using subgroup and the rest the no-tool subgroup, and the tool-using subgroup is all-wrong when none of its rollouts is correct, even if some no-tool rollouts in the same group succeed. Conditional on question where tool use is attempted, how often does the tool-using subgroup fail together? Figure 3b shows this all-wrong rate is 40% for tool-using subgroups compared to 25% for no-tool subgroups. Under group-normalized advantage, this asymmetry yields non-positive advantage on tool-call tokens in both regimes: in mixed groups they trail the no-tool success signal, and in fully all-wrong groups the numerator 𝑟𝑖 mean({𝑟𝑗}) collapses to zero. Either way, effective learning signal on tool calls is suppressed on the questions where tool use would matter most. Tool calls admit substantive resampling at fixed thinking. Resampling at the tool call is only worthwhile if it is real divergence point under the policy. If the thinking prefix already 4 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization commits to the concrete action, resampling at the tool-call boundary changes nothing. We probe this by decomposing trajectory at the first tool-call boundary: we fix its thinking prefix 𝑡1, draw 16 continuations from 𝜋𝜃( 𝑥, 𝑡1), and cluster the resulting tool calls into semantic classes by an LLM judge that groups calls by their semantic intent. Figure 3c shows that the resampled tool calls do not collapse to single class. Across all tools, the 16 samples occupy 2.93.4 distinct semantic clusters on average. The thinking prefix therefore does not determine the exact tool call and the tool call is an under-explored divergence point whose exploration must be supplied externally. Takeaways: GRPO under-trains the tool call Tool use remains minority behavior under GRPO: 2035% of rollouts across RL steps, well below the thinking-only majority (Figure 3a). When attempted, the tool-using subgroup is all-wrong on 40% of questions (vs. 25% for the no-tool subgroup), so tool-call tokens get negative advantage in mixed groups and zero in fully all-wrong groups (Figure 3b). Resampling from fixed prefix preserves substantive diversity in the tool call (2.93.4 semantic clusters per 16 samples in Figure 3c): the tool call is real divergence point. 3. Detailed analysis is in Appendix D. 3.2. Resample candidate selection Trigger and budget allocation. AXPO resamples only on groups in which the tool-using subgroup is non-empty and entirely wrong (an all-wrong tool-using subgroup; no-tool rollouts in the same group may succeed). These are exactly the groups where tool-call tokens receive non-positive advantage under GRPO (Section 2.2): zero when the entire group is all-wrong, negative when only no-tool rollouts succeed. recovered continuation therefore delivers the largest gradient lift per resampled continuation. Proposition 1 would in principle admit groups with at least one correct tool-using rollout as well, but those already produce positive advantage on tool-call tokens and gain comparatively little from marginal recovery. Resampling every triggered prefix would add up to 𝐵𝑁 𝐾 continuations on top of 𝐵𝑁 standard rollouts (batch size 𝐵), so AXPO caps the extra cost up to 𝑟 𝐵𝑁 per step with 𝑟 < 1 and allocates breadth-first: every triggered question receives its top-ranked prefix before any receives second. Uncertainty-based prefix ranking. Not every admitted prefix is worth resampling. Figure 3c shows 30% of prefixes collapse to single cluster across 16 resamples. AXPO therefore ranks candidates by the mean policy probability over the tool-call tokens in 𝜏 src (a tractable uncertainty proxy [34]) and resamples the lowest-confidence prefixes first (see Appendix B.9 for the choice of confidence over predictive entropy). 3.3. The recovery-indicator 𝑟prefix avoids the gradient conflict of propagating mixed-sign continuation advantages back to the shared prefix: ^𝐴prefix is monotone in 𝑟prefix, so the prefix is credited positively whenever at least one continuation succeeds, converting the coverage gain into gradient signal on the prefix tokens. 4. Experiments 4.1. Experimental setup Base models and tools. We use Qwen3-VL-Thinking [19] at three sizes (2B, 4B, 8B) as the base model across all methods. The tool set is the three families introduced in Section 2.1: Python interpreter, web search engine (Tavily search API1), and an image zoom-in tool. All tools share unified <tool_call> interface. Full prompt templates and tool schemas are in Appendix A.2. Training data & pipeline. SFT uses 64K correct trajectories drawn from ViRL [35], fvqa [36], and PyVision-RL [18], generated by Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking [19] where 25% contain tool call. RL uses 16K filtered subset plus 22K questions from MMFineReason-hard [37]. We follow the SFT then RL pipeline of Section 2.1. AXPO augments the rollout loop with tool-call resampling, holding all other hyperparameters constant against SFT + GRPO. Full details are in Appendix A.1. Benchmarks and metrics. We evaluate on nine multimodal benchmarks grouped into three task categories: Reasoning (MathVision [38], DynaMath [39], Math-VR [40]), Perception (V [8], VisualProbe [7], HR-Bench-4K and HR-Bench-8K [41]), and Search (HR-MMSearch [42], MMSearch [43]); per-benchmark descriptions in Appendix A.3. All benchmarks are evaluated in the agentic setting with the same tool interface used in training. We report Pass@1 and Pass@4 (%), averaged over four rollouts per question, with answer matching logic tailored for each benchmark. Baselines. We compare AXPO against four baselines: (1) Base: the base model only with prompting without additional training (2) SFT: the SFT checkpoint without RL (3) GRPO: RL from the base model without an SFT stage and (4) SFT + GRPO: the standard SFT-then-RL recipe. Main Results: AXPO narrows the agentic gap to 4 larger baseline. Table 1 reports Pass@1 across nine benchmarks at four model sizes (per-benchmark Pass@4 results in Table 5, Appendix B.2). SFT + AXPO outperforms SFT + GRPO at average performance on Pass@1 (+1.1 / +1.4 / +1.8 pp at 2B/4B/8B). On Pass@4, SFT + AXPO also beats SFT + GRPO at every scale (+2.8 / +2.3 / +1.8 pp). At 8B, SFT + AXPO reaches 99% of the 32B Bases Pass@1 and surpasses it on Pass@4 (75.8 vs. 75.1), despite having 4 fewer parameters. AXPO is the only recipe whose 8B point reaches the 32B line on Pass@1, and the only one whose Pass@4 crosses it (Figure 1). Decomposing the 8B Pass@1 column of Table 1, SFT + AXPO gains +0.6 pp on Reasoning, +2.9 pp on Perception, and +1.3 pp on Search over the SFT + GRPO baseline. In relative terms, the gain concentrates in Perception (+4%) and is smallest on Reasoning (+1%). Perception is where image-tool use most directly determines correctness, while on Reasoning the bottleneck is often the thinking rather than the tool call. detailed discussion of the results is provided in Appendix B.4. 4.3. Ablations: All components of AXPO matter Table 2 ablates each AXPO design choice on 8B. Per-row analysis is in Appendix B.6. 4.4. AXPO outperforms alternative RL recipes Table 3 compares AXPO against alternative training recipes: reward shaping (tool penalty / bonus), an extra rollout budget, and other RL algorithms (RLTF [46], CISPO [47], ARPO [31]). AXPO exceeds every alternative on both Pass@1 and Pass@4. The most direct more compute control, rollout 2, doubles rollout budget yet underperforms than AXPO. The gain therefore comes from where compute is spent, not from how much. Reward-shaping methods both fall below the GRPO baseline of 51.9, indicating the gap is not closed by reward shaping either in agentic reasoning. Detailed analysis is in Appendix B.7. 4.5. AXPO narrows the Thinking-Acting Gap during RL Training dynamics. Figure 4 (left & middle) tracks the two diagnostic symptoms of Section 2.2 over RL steps. Under SFT + GRPO, the tool-use rate stays flat near 30% and the all-wrong rate hovers around 40% throughout training. Under SFT + AXPO, tool-use climbs +28 pp over training and the all-wrong rate sits 17 pp below GRPO at every step, with tool-use rising during AXPO training while the all-wrong rate is suppressed by branching at each step, both consistent only under AXPO. The AXPO all-wrong rate is measured on the post-resampling subgroup composition (a tool-using subgroup that resampling recovers with at least one correct continuation no longer counts as all-wrong), so this persistent middle-panel gap is the per-step restore rate visualized in the right panel: tool-call resampling flips 12% of all-wrong tool-using subgroups per step into subgroups 9 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization Figure 4 Both Thinking-Acting Gap symptoms reverse during AXPO training but stay flat under GRPO, and the recovery does happen at resampling. Left & Middle: tool-use rate (top) and all-wrong rate on tool-using subgroups (bottom) over RL steps. Right: per-tool failed-subgroup recovery rate the share of all-wrong tool-using subgroups that resampling flips into subgroup with at least one correct tool-using trajectory. (a) Training stages on the tool-attempt rate vs. conditional pass@1 plane. Only AXPO expands both axes simultaneously. (b) Per-tool quality on the matched-tool-use subset (questions where both SFT+GRPO and SFT+AXPO invoke the tool). AXPO improves pass@1 and pass@4 across all three tool families. Figure 5 Post-training analysis pooled over MathVision, VisualProbe-Hard, and HR-MMSearch on 8B model. Conditional Pass@1 is measured on 715 tool-attempted questions from each benchmark. with at least one correct tool-call continuation. This rate is non-trivial because resampling targets the hardest cases where every original tool-using rollout already failed, and restores learning signal so the policy is rewarded for tool use. Test-time dynamics. More globally, Figure 5a positions each training stage on the (tool-attempt rate, conditional Pass@1) plane measured on three benchmarks: every other stage either trades tool frequency for quality (Base GRPO or SFT) or holds tool frequency fixed while pushing only quality (SFT SFT + GRPO), while only SFT + AXPO advances along both axes simultaneously. The post-training matched-tool analysis (Figure 5b) is the per-question analysis of this joint advance: on questions where SFT + GRPO and SFT + AXPO both invoke tool, AXPO improves Pass@1 across all three tool families, indicating the gain is in tool interaction quality. 5. Related Works Agentic reasoning for VLMs. Agentic reasoning extends the reasoning loop beyond the models token space, allowing it to emit tool call, receive an external output, and condition subsequent reasoning on it [48, 9]. Tools span knowledge search [5, 4], computation [10, 11, 4951], and visual perception [44, 7, 12, 45, 52, 53]. Recent VLM systems combine image manipulation with code and search [43, 23, 18, 14, 54], and follow two-stage SFT-then-RL recipe. Agent Explorative Policy Optimization RL for agentic reasoning. On thinking-only trajectories, group-relative methods like GRPO [24 26] normalize rewards within 𝑁 rollouts and uniformly assign the resulting advantage; BroRL [30] analyzes how scaling 𝑁 closes coverage gaps. Three lines extend this family to tool-using rollouts: direct ports that reuse outcome-reward RL without changing the sampling structure [45, 23, 55 58, 22, 42], mid-trajectory branching that resamples the continuation after tool call to absorb tool-output uncertainty [31, 59], and reward shaping that adds bonus or penalty terms on tool use [18, 29]. We include more detailed comparison with related works in Appendix C. Our contribution. AXPO contrasts with each line. Against direct use of GRPO, it restores learning signal on the all-wrong tool-using subgroups identified in Section 2.2, which uniform sampling cannot reach. Against mid-trajectory branching [31, 59], it resamples at the tool-call boundary rather than after the tool observation, exploring the tool call itself rather than the continuation given tool output. Against reward shaping, it acts on the rollout distribution while leaving the reward intact. 6. Conclusion We identified Thinking-Acting Gap in group-based agentic RL, an asymmetry between thinking (the self-contained default) and tool use (a high-variance auxiliary) that manifests during training as two symptoms: tool use is attempted on minority of rollouts and tool-using subgroups (the tool-using rollouts within group) are disproportionately all-wrong, so the advantage at tool-call tokens is non-positive on exactly the questions where tool use would help. AXPO narrows this gap by augmenting the rollout loop with tool-call resampling, which fixes the thinking prefix and concentrates exploration on the tool call itself, paired with uncertainty-based prefix selection. Across nine multimodal benchmarks and three model scales, AXPO consistently outperforms SFT + GRPO, and at 8B it surpasses the 32B Base baseline on Pass@4 with 4 fewer parameters. Limitations. Our study assumes verifiable outcome rewards for RL and trains models up to 8B parameters. We discuss limitations and the future directions in details in Appendix E. 11 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization

Experimental Setup This section expands on Section 4.1: training data and hyperparameters (Appendix A.1), the agentic system prompt and tool schemas (Appendix A.2), and per-benchmark descriptions (Appendix A.3). A.1. Training We expand on Section 4.1 with the full training data composition and hyperparameter list. SFT data composition. SFT uses 64,274 trajectories drawn from three sources: ViRL [35], fvqa [36], and PyVision-RL [18]. All trajectories are generated by Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking [19] acting as the teacher model. 25% of the trajectories contain at least one tool call; the remainder solve the problem with internal reasoning alone. We retain only trajectories whose final answer is correct against the ground-truth label. RL data composition. RL uses 15,591 problems obtained by filtering the SFT problem set: we remove problems that the SFT checkpoint solves on all 4 rollouts, and problems that the 32B teacher fails on all 4 rollouts. This filtering convention follows prior agentic RL work [23, 26] and removes both trivial problems (zero learning signal) and unreachable problems (no positive signal achievable at this scale). We additionally include 22,000 hard questions from MMFineReason-hard2 to expose the policy to problems unseen during SFT, bringing the RL training set to 37K problems. Policy gradient objective. Both SFT + GRPO and SFT + AXPO maximize the standard PPOclip surrogate [32] regularized by KL penalty toward fixed reference policy 𝜋ref (the SFT-initialized checkpoint). Letting 𝜌𝑡(𝜃) = 𝜋𝜃(𝑦𝑡 𝑥, 𝑦<𝑡)/𝜋𝜃old (𝑦𝑡 𝑥, 𝑦<𝑡) denote the per-token importance ratio and ^𝐴 the trajectory-level advantage broadcast uniformly to every against the rollout policy 𝜋𝜃old token of 𝑦, ℒclip(𝑦; ^𝐴) = E𝑡 [ min(𝜌𝑡(𝜃) ^𝐴, clip(𝜌𝑡(𝜃), 1 𝜖low, 1 + 𝜖high) ^𝐴) (6) 𝛽 𝐷KL[𝜋𝜃( 𝑥, 𝑦<𝑡) 𝜋ref( 𝑥, 𝑦<𝑡)] ]. ℒclip(𝑦; ^𝐴)]. GRPO instantiates ^𝐴 = 𝐴𝑖 for trajectory 𝜏𝑖 The training objective is max𝜃 E𝑦𝜋𝜃old from Section 2.1; AXPO applies the same surrogate per stream in Equation 5 (per-prefix advantage on continuations, recovery-indicator advantage on the source prefix). We use the asymmetric DAPO clipping range 𝜖low = 0.2, 𝜖high = 0.4 and KL coefficient 𝛽 = 1 103. [ Hyperparameters. We adopt the ProRL [26] recipe for the RL stage. The key hyperparameters are: Sampling temperature: 1.0 KL coefficient: 1 103 Asymmetric clipping: 𝜖low = 0.2, 𝜖high = 0.4 (DAPO-style [25]) Group size: 𝑁 = 8 rollouts per question Maximum number of turns per trajectory: 3 Hardware: 8 A100 80GB GPUs 2A question subset of the MMFineReason corpus that Qwen3-VL-4B entirely fails on 4 rollouts. 21 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization All hyperparameters are held constant between SFT + GRPO and SFT + AXPO; only the rollout loop differs (see below). Per-prefix resample count: 𝐾 = 4 continuations per selected prefix. A.2. System Prompt and Tool Interface We use the same agentic system prompt for SFT, RL, and evaluation across all model sizes where the prompt is modified version of the system prompt of deepeyes v2 [23]. Tools are exposed via unified <tool_call> interface used in qwen-agent [19] and the model is free to invoke any subset of the three tools (python, tavily-search, image_zoom_in) on every question. The full text of the prompt is reproduced below. System Prompt You are an agent: please keep going until the users query is completely resolved, before ending your turn and yielding back to the user. Only terminate your turn when you are sure that the problem is solved. Solve the following problem step by step. In your reasoning process, if the answer cannot be determined, you can write Python code in Jupyter Notebook to process the image and extract more information from it. The stdout and stderr content, along with the images generated by plt.show() will be returned to better assist with the user query. You MUST use the python tool to analyze or transform images whenever it could improve your understanding. This includes but is not limited to zooming in, rotating, adjusting contrast, computing statistics, or isolating features. If you find you lack sufficient knowledge to confidently answer the question, you MUST conduct search to thoroughly seek the internet for information. No matter how complex the query, you will not give up until you find the corresponding information. You can use the tavily-search tool, which will search the web and return relevant information based on your query. Notes for tools: ## image_zoom_in tool 1. image_zoom_in crops and magnifies specific region of the image so you can inspect fine details. 2. When you need to look at specific region more closely, prefer image_zoom_in over writing python code to crop it is simpler, faster, and returns high-resolution view directly. 3. After zoom-in, the cropped image is appended to _pil_images in the python tool (the observation will tell you the exact index), so you can further process it with code if needed. ## python tool 1. python runs in stateful Jupyter notebook kernel variables, imports, and state persist across calls. 2. The conversation images are pre-loaded as _pil_images (list of PIL Image objects) and _images (list of file paths). Images produced by other tools (e.g. image_zoom_in) are also appended to these lists. Agent Explorative Policy Optimization 3. Use plt.show() to visualize processed images; generated plots are automatically captured and returned. 4. Available packages include numpy, scipy, sympy, matplotlib, PIL/Pillow, and the standard library. 5. Always use Python for non-trivial computation trigonometric calculations, multistep arithmetic, equation solving, and area/volume formulas. Do NOT compute these mentally; write code to ensure numerical accuracy. 6. Your program should always return in finite time. Do not write infinite loops. 7. Writing file to disk is not allowed. ## tavily-search tool 1. You MUST engage in many interactions, delving deeply into the topic to explore all possible aspects until satisfactory answer is found. 2. Before presenting Final Answer, you will cross-check and validate the information youve gathered to confirm its accuracy and reliability. 3. You will carefully analyze each information source to ensure that all data is current, relevant, and from credible origins. ## general You MUST think and plan before each function call, and reflect on the outcomes of the previous function calls. DO NOT do this entire process by making function calls only, as this can impair your ability to solve the problem and think insightfully. Remember to place the final answer in the last part using the format: <answer> boxed{"The final answer goes here."} </answer> Tool schemas. Each tool is registered with the model using the OpenAI function-calling schema (name, description, JSON parameters) and is concatenated with the system prompt above at every step. To prevent answer leakage during training and evaluation, tavily-search is configured with the API-level parameter exclude_domains = ["huggingface.co"], which filters out the site most likely to index benchmark questions or solutions. The exclusion is enforced uniformly across training and evaluation rollouts. A.3. Benchmark Details We evaluate on nine multimodal benchmarks grouped by which tool family is most expected to help: Reasoning (Python interpreter), Perception (image zoom-in), and Search (web search). All benchmarks are evaluated in the agentic setting with the same tool interface used in training; the model is free to use any subset of the three tools or none at all on every question. Reasoning. These benchmarks evaluate multi-step reasoning over text, math, and figures, where Python interpreter is the natural tool for symbolic and numerical computation. MathVision [38]: competition-style math problems paired with diagrams (geometry, algebra, combinatorics). Answers are short numerical or symbolic expressions; we use the public mini test set containing 304 questions. DynaMath [39]: benchmark of programmatically generated math questions with multiple parametric variants per seed problem. We evaluate on single-variant slice (501 seed problems). The benchmark complements MathVision as it provides additional algorithmically generated problems spanning plane geometry, statistics, and other domains with diverse difficulty. Math-VR [40]: mathematical visual reasoning benchmark introduced with CodePlot-CoT, in which problems are paired with diagrams (figures, plots, geometric constructions) that the model must inspect to compute the answer. The benchmark targets the regime where the visual content is load-bearing for the math derivation rather than decorative. Perception. These benchmarks contain high-resolution images with information that is hard to read at the default vision encoder resolution. The image zoom-in tool is the natural recovery mechanism. [8]: fine-grained visual-search benchmark in which the answer depends on small object embedded in complex scene. Designed to expose the failure of monolithic image encoders on high-resolution detail. Agent Explorative Policy Optimization VisualProbe [7]: multimodal probing benchmark introduced with mini-o3, where each question targets specific small region (text, sign, label) within high-resolution scene. We use the hard subset, which selects questions Qwen3-VL-Thinking-8B fails without tools. HR-Bench-4K and HR-Bench-8K [41]: high-resolution single-image perception at 40962 and 81922 resolutions, respectively. Questions probe small text, distant objects, and fine textures; the two splits share common question pool with images rescaled to the target resolution. Search. These benchmarks require external knowledge that is unlikely to be in the models parametric memory or that requires multi-hop lookup. The Tavily web search API is the natural tool. HR-MMSearch [42]: high-resolution multimodal search questions whose answers are composed by chaining two or more web lookups (e.g., in which year was the mayor of the city where this fashion designer was born). The benchmark stresses both perception (identifying entities from the image) and multi-hop search composition. MMSearch [43]: an earlier multimodal search benchmark covering current-events questions whose answers depend on web content released after the models training cutoff. Predominantly single-hop, but answers are often time-sensitive. Evaluation protocol. For all benchmarks, we draw four rollouts per question and report Pass@1 (per-question accuracy averaged over four rollouts) and Pass@4 (a question is correct if any of the four rollouts is correct). Final-answer matching against the gold label is performed by an exact match for math and multiple-choice question and F1 score for search questions. Post-training is therefore necessary at every trainable scale. Why SFT before RL? Comparing GRPO (RL from base) against SFT + GRPO measures the value of an SFT initialization before RL. Pass@1 gaps shrink with size (+5.3 at 2B, +5.2 at 4B, +1.3 at 8B), but Pass@4 gaps remain positive at every scale (+6.8, +4.9, +2.4). RL from the base policy can find single high-reward mode (Pass@1 partially recovered at 8B), but it consistently sacrifices diversity, capping the Pass@4 ceiling [62]. SFT broadens the cold-start distribution over tool-using trajectories before group-based exploration begins. Without it, all-wrong tool-using subgroups dominate from step 1 and the policy collapses onto narrow single-mode solution [23]. it Why RL after SFT? SFT minimizes log-likelihood on fixed set of teacher trajectories: can make the student imitate the teachers tool-use patterns, but the optimization signal lives entirely on the teachers distribution. At test time the student samples from its own distribution and small token-level deviations from the teacher accumulate into prefixes the SFT objective never scored, so imitation alone does not guarantee that on-policy rollouts inherit the teachers correctness (exposure bias). RL after SFT narrows this gap by training on the policys own rollouts under an outcome reward, adapting the policy to its own induced trajectory distribution rather than to the teachers [63]. Empirically, comparing SFT against SFT + GRPO isolates this on-policy adaptation effect given the SFT initialization: Pass@1 improves at every scale (+4.5 at 2B, +1.2 at 4B, +2.0 at 8B), the regime where on-policy reliability matters most, while Pass@4 gains are smaller and uneven (+2.3, +0.0, +1.8), consistent with GRPO consolidating mass on trajectories the SFT policy can already reach rather than discovering new ones. RL is therefore necessary on top of SFT, but standard GRPOs adaptation is biased toward the SFT manifold, motivating AXPOs tool-call resampling. 29 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization Table 7 Non-agent vs Base: effect of turning tools on without any training, across nine multimodal benchmarks and four model sizes. Why AXPO on top of SFT, instead of GRPO? The previous two paragraphs leave structural gap: SFT sets the Pass@4 ceiling and RL sharpens within it, so further gains require mechanism that simultaneously extends the ceiling and sharpens. AXPO does both. Pass@1 gains over SFT + GRPO are +1.1/ +1.4/ +1.8 at 2B/4B/8B, and Pass@4 gains are +2.8/ +2.3/ +1.8. The Pass@4 lift is the diagnostic signature that AXPO operates on different axis from SFT-then-GRPO sharpening: by recovering all-wrong tool-using subgroups whose gradient signal would otherwise vanish (Section 2.2), AXPO turns previously dead continuations into trainable ones, expanding the policys reachable correct-trajectory set rather than only redistributing mass within it. B.6. Detailed Ablation Analysis Table 2 ablates each AXPO design choice on 8B at matched compute. All numbers below are 8B Pass@1 averages on the four-benchmark composition (MathVision, V, VisualProbe, HR-MMSearch). Prefix fixing. Removing prefix fixing reduces AXPO to 52.1, near the SFT + GRPO baseline of 51.9: each resample becomes fresh rollout from 𝜋𝜃( 𝑥), so only 𝑞-fraction of resamples are tool-using and the (1𝑞) waste factor that Proposition 1 removes returns in full. Tool-using resampling target. Targeting questions where all no-tool rollouts fail for resampling instead reaches 51.7, since the policy already covers the no-tool subgroup under GRPO and additional samples there cannot recover the missing tool-using signal. Resampling pays off only when the resampling target is the under-covered tool-using subgroup. Uncertainty-based prefix selection. Replacing uncertainty rank with random ranking drops to 51.0 (2.9 pp): the prefix is still fixed so the coverage gain is preserved, but random ranking routes the budget to prefixes where the policy has already converged toward wrong continuation, wasting resamples on determined commitments. 31 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization Prefix credit. Dropping the recovery-indicator credit on the source prefix yields 51.4 (2.5 pp): prefix tokens receive no positive signal even when resampling recovers continuation, so the policy is not steered toward producing high-yield prefixes in the first place. Per-prefix advantage group. Merging resampled continuations into the source group instead of forming per-prefix advantage yields 51.2 (2.7 pp): the shared prefix then collects contradictory advantages from the failed source and successful resamples while no-tool successes in the source group dilute the recovered signal. B.7. Detailed Comparison Analysis Table 3 compares AXPO to alternative training recipes. All numbers are 8B Pass@1 averages. Reward shaping does not recover the gain of AXPO. tool penalty [29] drops to 46.0 and tool bonus [18, 44, 45] reaches only 50.8, both below the GRPO baseline of 51.9. Reward-channel interventions either suppress tool use globally (penalty) or reward tool calls regardless of whether they help (bonus); neither addresses the coverage problem that AXPO targets, which is conditional on the prefix being tool-using and on resampling restoring at least one correct continuation. Extra rollout budget does not close the gap. Doubling the GRPO rollout count (4 exceeding AXPOs extra compute) reaches 52.4, still 1.5 pp below SFT + AXPO. The extra budget re-derives non-tool-using prefixes and cannot break the all-wrong tool-using subgroups that resampling is designed to recover; throwing more samples at the same (1𝑞) waste factor does not change the structural problem. Alternative RL algorithms. We replace GRPO with three recently proposed alternatives. RLTF [46], an algorithm that utilizes the text feedback from the external teacher, reaches only 43.3 Avg with 40% tool utilization, suffering tool-call collapse over training (consistent with reports of hint leakage degrading text-feedback RL on agentic tasks). CISPO [47], an importance-sampling variant, reaches 47.8 Avg: its clip rule reduces gradient bias but does not address the all-wrong subgroup whose advantage is zero by construction. ARPO [31], an agent-specific branching method that draws additional continuations after tool observation when post-observation entropy is high, is the closest comparison and the strongest competitor at 51.3 Avg, yet still trails AXPO by 2.6 pp: post-observation branching cannot recover when the tool call itself is the failure mode, which is the regime Section 2.2 identifies as binding. The RL-algorithm sweep confirms AXPOs gain is not generic effect of any agent-aware RL trick but specifically reflects tool-call resamplings coverage restoration on tool-using thinking prefixes. B.8. B.9. Confidence as Tractable Proxy for Tool-Call Entropy Section 3 ranks candidate prefixes by the mean probability the source policy assigned to the tool-call tokens of 𝜏 src (henceforth confidence). The natural alternative is the predictive entropy 𝐻(𝜋𝜃( 𝑥, 𝑦<𝑡)) of the next-token distribution at each tool-call position, but this choice is precluded by training-loop structure. Per-token sampling probabilities are emitted essentially for free by the rollout engine (vLLM [65]), whereas predictive entropy requires either an extra forward pass over the full vocabulary under the trained policy (FSDP) or vLLM-side full-vocabulary logprob expansion at decode time. Our RL pipeline under verl [60] alternates rollout (vLLM) and gradient updates (FSDP), so inserting per-prefix entropy computation would force an additional vLLM FSDP vLLM round-trip per training step (or equivalently expensive vLLM-side full-vocabulary logprob expansion), cost we wished to avoid. To verify that confidence preserves the ranking an entropy-based scheme would induce, we measured the per-step correlation between mean-token-confidence and exact policy entropy on tool-call positions of failed tool-using rollouts collected during training (Figure 6). Across training steps, the two signals are strongly correlated (Pearson 𝜌 = 0.843, Spearman 𝜌 = 0.835, OLS fit 𝑦 = 0.41𝑥 + 0.07 on entropy vs negated confidence). Confidence therefore preserves the relative ordering an entropy-based scheme would produce, and we adopt it as the ranking signal in AXPO. C. Extended Related Works This appendix expands on Section 5 with the longer descriptions that the main text abridges. 33 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization Figure 6 Confidence vs. exact policy entropy at tool-call positions. For each training step, we plot the mean confidence (vLLM rollout, free) against the exact policy entropy (FSDP forward, full vocabulary) on tool-call tokens of failed tool-using rollouts. The two signals are strongly correlated (Pearson 𝜌 = 0.843, Spearman 𝜌 = 0.835), justifying confidence as tractable uncertainty proxy. Two-stage SFT-then-RL recipe for agentic VLMs. Recent agentic VLM systems share twostage recipe: supervised fine-tuning on tool-using trajectories distilled from larger teachers [66, 20], followed by outcome-reward RL [18, 23, 45]. They differ mainly in the distillation corpus, the tool interface, and the choice of base model. Most prior agentic-RL VLM studies focus on single tool family (typically image manipulation), whereas our setup combines code, search, and image zoom-in on thinking-capable base (Qwen3-VL-Thinking) [19], configuration under which the RL stage becomes the primary bottleneck. Group-relative RL and BroRL. RL with verifiable outcome rewards is the standard post-SFT stage for reasoning models [1, 2]. GRPO [2426] samples 𝑁 rollouts per question, normalizes rewards within the group, and assigns the resulting advantage uniformly to every token. BroRL [30] argues that increasing 𝑁 reduces unsampled blind spots at rate determined by per-token baseline probability. Both analyses were developed for thinking-only trajectories, where the rollout structure is uniform across tokens and the per-token baseline probability decays slowly. Proposition 1 shows that AXPOs prefix-fixed resampling dominates raw scaling in the regime where tool-call tokens sit at low policy probability, exactly the failure mode Section 2.2 diagnoses. Mid-trajectory branching for tool-using RL. ARPO [31] and AEPO [59] adapt group-relative RL to tool-using rollouts by branching after tool observation, drawing additional continuations from 𝜋𝜃( 𝑥, 𝑡1, 𝑎1, 𝑜1) when post-observation entropy is high. The exploration target is the continuation given fixed tool call, which absorbs variance introduced by the tool output but cannot recover when the tool call itself is wrong (wrong sub-image, wrong python code, wrong search query). AXPO instead branches at the tool-call boundary and triggers only on all-wrong tool-using subgroups, with per-prefix advantage that credits the source prefix exactly when its branched continuation succeeds, making it targeted intervention on the regime Section 2.2 identifies as binding rather than general rollout-budget reshaper. Empirically, AXPO outperforms ARPO on the 8B comparison (53.9 vs. 51.3 Avg, Table 3), consistent with the diagnosis that the tool call itself, not the post-observation continuation, is the dominant failure locus. The two strategies are compatible, and combining 34 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization tool-call and post-observation branching is natural extension we leave to future work. D. Our target is tool use, and the diagnostics in Section 2.2 establish exactly the regime where naive rollout scaling fails to help: tool use is rare (Figure 3a: 𝑞 [0.2, 0.35]) and allwrong rates on tool-using subgroups are high (Figure 3b), so tool-call tokens sit in low-probability regime under the policy. Hu et al. [30] show that scaling 𝑁 reduces unsampled blind spots at rate 𝑝2(1 𝑝)𝑁 (Theorem 1, Lemma 2); this decay is per-token, fast for high-𝑝 regions and slow for low-𝑝 regions. BroRLs guarantee is uniform in 1 token type; AXPO targets the specific region that the low tool-use attempt rate has made hardest to cover. E. Limitations and Future Work We expand on the limitations sketched in the main-text conclusion and discuss future directions. Verifiable outcome rewards. AXPOs subgroup-level trigger (all-wrong tool-using subgroup) and per-prefix advantage in Eq. 2 both rely on binary, automatically verifiable outcome signal 𝑟 {0, 1}. Tasks where verifiability is partial (open-ended generation [67, 68], multi-criterion subjective evaluation) require different definition of failed subgroup before tool-call resampling applies. The resampling mechanism itself is reward-agnostic, but the trigger and the all-wrong learning signal argument in Section 2.2 are not. Compute scope of training. Trained models are at most 8B parameters; 32B is reported inferenceonly as the SFT teacher. This boundary reflects available compute rather than methodological choice [69, 68, 7072]. Whether the Thinking-Acting Gap and AXPOs recovery generalize to 32B+ trainable scale, where the base policy is closer to the tool-use ceiling and the all-wrong-subgroup fraction may be smaller, is open. Furthermore, although the teacher model can theoretically scale to 100B+ parameters, analyzing the effects of teacher scaling on downstream reinforcement learning (RL) remains an area for future work. Future work: richer tool environments. Our tool inventory covers three families that span numerical, retrieval, and visual modalities (Python interpreter, web search, image zoom-in), each shown to be load-bearing in prior agentic VLM work [23, 18, 7]; this multi-tool setup is already broader than most prior agentic-VLM RL studies, which typically focus on single tool family. One notable absence is an image-search tool, which prior works [23, 42] report results with but which we could not include during training and full evaluation due to per-call API costs at our scale (we instead approximate it via GPT-5.4 prompt for the unseen-tool study in Appendix B.5). Tools with longer execution latency (browser agents, GUI control, terminal sessions) and tools with 36 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization high per-call cost (LLM-as-tool) [13] introduce additional considerations that we do not address. Extending AXPO to such richer [7375], longer-horizon environments is natural next step. Future work: generalization to other base models. All experiments use Qwen3-VL-Thinking, which is currently the strongest open thinking-capable VLM with reliable tool-using ability and the model best supported by our RL framework. The Thinking-Acting Gap is property of RL on an agentic reasoning model rather than of any specific architecture [7679], and we expect the diagnostics and AXPOs coverage gain to transfer; replicating on the most recent Qwen3.5 models [80], once RL framework support matures, would help establish the gap as general phenomenon and rule out base-specific confounds. F. Broader Impacts AXPO is methodological improvement to the RL training of agentic VLMs and is additive on top of publicly released base model (Qwen3-VL-Thinking [19]), so it does not introduce new capability class. Among the three tool families used in this work, the Python interpreter raises standard security concerns for code-executing agents. We can run all tool calls in sandboxed execution environment during both training and evaluation, consistent with prior agentic-VLM RL work [12]. Beyond this sandboxing, broader safety considerations [8187] (e.g., misuse of tool-grounded outputs, causality, robustness, deployment-time guardrails, downstream effects of cheaper agentic deployments) are out of scope for this work and were not addressed. 37 Agent Explorative Policy Optimization G.
